title: Forecasting Dengue: Journey Through Challenges, Actions, and Lessons Learned
text:
Sri Lanka Climate
🌴Climate type:

Tropical climate: Warm and humid throughout the year

☀️Average temperature:

Typically 26°C – 30°C in lowlands
Cooler in central highlands (e.g., Nuwara Eliya ~16°C–20°C)

🌧💧️ Rainfall:

Two main monsoon seasons:

Southwest monsoon (May–September)
Northeast monsoon (December–February)

How do different training dataset choices and different model choices influence forecast error?
Do training dataset and model choices exhibit spatial patterns across districts?
Are the strengths of seasonality and the strengths of trend, computed over different time periods, helpful in identifying the best-performing combination of training dataset and model?
Do the strength of seasonality and the strength of trend, computed over different time periods, help explain the choice of the best model and training dataset?

title: Forecasting Dengue: Journey Through Challenges, Actions, and Lessons Learned
text:
Training Dataset Choices

All data
Use only post COVID-19 period data
Forecasting the interrupted period first and then using it for modeling
Down-weight the interrupted period

title: Forecasting Dengue: Journey Through Challenges, Actions, and Lessons Learned
text:
Model Choices

Automated ARIMA algorithms
Trigonometric seasonality, Box-Cox transformation, ARMA errors, Trend and Seasonal components (TBATS) model
STL - ETS approach: Using STL decomposition, the seasonal component is extracted from the original series. Then, the ETS model is fitted to the seasonally adjusted series. When fitting the ETS model, the seasonal component is set to “None”. In this way, STL captures seasonality, ETS models the remaining structure, and the final forecast is their combination.
Dynamic Harmonic Regression (DHR): Complex seasonality is modeled using Fourier terms combined with ARIMA errors. When the seasonal cycle is long (e.g., 52 weeks), DHR models usually perform better than ARIMA or ETS models.

title: Forecasting Dengue: Journey Through Challenges, Actions, and Lessons Learned
text:
Time Series Features
strength of the trend
\[\begin{equation}
F_T = \max \left(0,\; 1 - \frac{\operatorname{Var}(R_t)}{\operatorname{Var}(T_t + R_t)} \right)
\end{equation}\]
strength of seasonality \[\begin{equation}
F_S = \max \left(0,\; 1 - \frac{\operatorname{Var}(R_t)}{\operatorname{Var}(S_t + R_t)} \right)
\end{equation}\]
\(\operatorname{Var}(R_t)\) - variance of the remainder component
\(\operatorname{Var}(T_t + R_t)\) - variance of the detrended series
\(\operatorname{Var}(S_t + R_t)\) - variance after removing the seasonal component.

title: Forecasting Dengue: Journey Through Challenges, Actions, and Lessons Learned
text:
Lessons Learned

There is no single best combination of training dataset and forecasting model that consistently yields the best performance for a given time series.
This suggests that early warning systems should be adaptive rather than static, and should incorporate mechanisms to detect and respond to structural changes in disease transmission dynamics.

title: Forecasting Dengue: Journey Through Challenges, Actions, and Lessons Learned
text:
Action 4: Two-step forecasting model
Step 1: What does the data suggest will happen?
\[\hat{Y_t} = f(\text{climate},\text{time lags},\text{urbanization}, \text{mobility}, \text{interventions})\]
Step 2: Does reality suggest something the model cannot see yet?
\[
\hat{Y_t}^* =
g\left(
\text{prediction from step 1},
\substack{\text{clinical and epidemiological} \\
          \text{domain knowledge}}
\right)
\]

title: Forecasting Dengue: Journey Through Challenges, Actions, and Lessons Learned
text:
Lessons Learned

Local epidemiological knowledge can identify risks before they appear in the surveillance data.
Human expertise and statistical models should complement, not replace, each other.
Effective forecasting requires continuous monitoring, evaluation, and adaptation.
Collaboration among epidemiologists, entomologists, clinicians, public health officials, and data scientists is essential.

title: Forecasting Dengue: Journey Through Challenges, Actions, and Lessons Learned
